content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

September 6, 2017

Dr. Sean Wright
Mayor of Antioch
The Wright Start Family Wellness Center
3432 Hillcrest Avenue, Suite 200
Antioch, CA 94531

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-17-201

Dear Mayor Wright:

This letter responds to your request for advice as Mayor of the City of Antioch regarding your duties under the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

QUESTION

If your office property is 700 feet from the closest location of the proposed property to be developed, may you participate in the hearing and vote on the project if and/or when it comes before the Antioch City Council?

CONCLUSION

Based on the significant change in use of the undeveloped property within 700 feet of your property, and potential effects on traffic levels in the area, you would have a conflict of interest in the decisions.

FACTS

DeNova Homes, a local housing and commercial developer in your region, is proposing a new mixed-use development (single-family detached homes, condominiums and commercial buildings) on the currently vacant plot of land between Hillcrest Avenue, Wildflower Drive and

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-201
Page No. 2

Terrace View Avenue in the City of Antioch (the "Project"). According to the City Planning
Division's "Project Pipeline" on the City's website,² the project will involve 23.03 acres, to be
developed with 10.45 acres of commercial use, 7.00 acres of condominiums (98 units), and 4.5
acres of single family (22 lots).³

You are the Mayor of the City of Antioch and in your private capacity are a longtime
business owner/chiropractor in the City of Antioch. You own a condominium (one unit in the
building) down the street from the development site that you use as a chiropractic office where you
see patients. You estimate that the distance from your chiropractic office to the closest corner of the
development site is 700 feet.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using
his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial
interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the official's
interests set forth in Section 87103. Pertinent to your question are the following:

• Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth
$2,000 or more or holds a position as a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds
any position of management. (Section 87103(a) and Section 87103(d).)

• Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000
or more. (Section 87103(b).)

• Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending institution made in the
regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status,
aggregating $500 or more in value provided or promised to, received by, the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. (Section 87103(c).)

Your chiropractic practice is a business entity in which you have an investment interest, at which
you hold a business position and receive income. (Section 87102(a)(c) and (d).) Your clients are
also considered sources of income, however, you have not asked about any of your clients.

Foreseeability and Materiality

A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
governmental decision on a public official's interest is material. (Section 87100.) The standard for
foreseeability differs depending on whether an interest is explicitly involved in the decision.
(Regulation 18701.) An interest is explicitly involved in a decision if the interest is a named party
in, or the subject of, the governmental decision, or and the decision affects real property as
described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(1)-(6). (Regulation 18701(a).) Under the facts you have

² http://www.ci.antioch.ca.us/CityGov/CommDev/PlanningDivision/docs/Project-Pipeline.pdf

³ On August 18, 2017, you provided the additional maps and diagrams of the project.

File No. A-17-201
Page No. 3

provided, your interests are not explicitly involved in any of the decisions. Thus, as applied to each
official's respective real property interest, Regulation 18701(b) provides: "[i]n general, if the
financial effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical,
it is reasonably foreseeable."

A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
governmental decision on a public official's interests is material.

Real Property

As relevant to the facts here, the financial effect of the decisions on the official's respective
real property interests is material if the decisions:

• Would change the character of the parcel of real property by substantially altering traffic
levels or intensity of use, including parking, of property surrounding the official's real
property parcel, the view, privacy, noise levels, or air quality, including odors, or any other
factors that would affect the market value of the real property parcel in which the official
has a financial interest. (Regulation 18702.2(a)(10).)

• Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and consideration under the
circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision was of such a nature that its
reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the market value of the official's property.
(Regulation 18702.2(a)(12).)

We note that according to the City Planning Division's "Project Pipeline" the project will involve
23.03 acres, to be developed with 10.45 acres of commercial use, seven acres of condominiums (98
units), and 4.5 acres of single family (22 lots). This site is currently undeveloped. A reasonably
prudent person would believe, based on the significant change in use of the undeveloped property,
that the decision would affect the market value of surrounding properties, even those 700 feet away.
Additionally, such a large project would likely substantially alter traffic levels. Thus, the effect on
the property would be material.

Because we have concluded that you have a conflict of interest based on your real property
interest, we do not further analyze potential effects on your business interests.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Jack C. Woodside
General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Assistant General Counsel
Legal Division

JWW:jgl

developed, may you participate in the hearing and vote on the project if and/or when it comes 
before the Antioch City Council?

CONCLUSION: Based on the significant change in use of the undeveloped property within 700 feet of your 
property, and potential effects on traffic levels in the area, you would have a conflict of interest in
